judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with solid grounding; Report 2 is slightly more decisive on the AI-assistance footer (unambiguous "should be deleted") while Report 1 hedges it as an arbitration call, but overall the two are comparable in substance and accuracy.

report_1.evidence: "Two-factor authentication is now enabled for every clinic by default" — false.** `app/security/config.py` sets `TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT = False` with the comment "Two-factor authentication is opt-in per clinic." ... Remove it or rewrite it as opt-in. ... "Patients can now export their full visit history as a PDF" — not implemented.** `app/routers/visits.py` contains only a `/visits` list route and the comment `# TODO: pdf export`. There is no export code anywhere in the app. ... Remove this section.

report_2.evidence: "Patients can now export their full visit history as a PDF" (lines 22–26) — remove; the feature does not exist.** `app/routers/visits.py` contains only a `/visits` endpoint returning `[]` and the comment `# TODO: pdf export` ... "Two-factor authentication is now enabled for every clinic by default" (line 30) — false; correct or remove.** `app/security/config.py` sets `TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT = False`, with the comment "Two-factor authentication is opt-in per clinic."

report_1.evidence: `before_scheduling.png` shows the scheduling calendar (it renders "SCHEDULING CALENDAR"), but `after_scheduling.png` renders "BILLING / INVOICES" — and the capture manifest confirms it: `captures.json` records `after_scheduling.png` as captured from route `/billing/invoices`, while the before was captured from `/schedule`.

report_2.evidence: **The "after" image is not the scheduling calendar at all.** `img/after_scheduling.png` visibly reads "BILLING / INVOICES", while `img/before_scheduling.png` reads "SCHEDULING CALENDAR". The repo's own capture manifest confirms this: `docs/release/captures.json` records `after_scheduling.png` as captured from route `/billing/invoices`, versus `/schedule` for the before shot.

report_1.evidence: **Referenced but missing:** `img/after_export.png` (line 26 of the doc). No such file exists, and `captures.json` itself flags it: "expected capture; no corresponding file." ... **On disk but never referenced:** `img/unused_capture_07.png`, which renders "PATIENT LIST." It also does not appear in `captures.json`, so it is an orphan on both counts.

report_2.evidence: `img/after_export.png` | **No — missing** | `captures.json` itself flags it: "expected capture; no corresponding file"... Conversely, one file on disk is referenced by nothing: `img/unused_capture_07.png`, which shows a "PATIENT LIST" screen and is absent from both the document and `captures.json`.

report_1.evidence: **"Highlights draft assembled with AI assistance."** (line 33). Two problems: the word "draft" is an internal status marker that should never appear in the sent version, and the AI-attribution footer is a tooling artifact whose inclusion in customer-facing material is the repository owner's call. ... but as written ("draft") it cannot ship.

report_2.evidence: **The trailing line "Highlights draft assembled with AI assistance." (line 33)** is an internal production note. Whatever the clinic's stance on AI disclosure, this drafting-process footnote (with a horizontal rule above it) was not written for customers and should be deleted before sending.
